context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m kinda lost on the spider diagram thing for my city case study 😭 can u just explain the whole assignment from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Hey! Let's work through this together. Before I jump in, tell me: what city did you pick for your case study, and have you had a chance to look at the Spider Diagram template or the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation of the assignment, which would be doing too much for them. Instead, I should acknowledge their confusion, but redirect them to engage actively with the assignment themselves. I'll ask them a targeted question to find out what they already know, so I can gauge where to start. This keeps the interaction Socratic and student-driven.

exchanges.student: uhh I picked Jakarta, but idk what I’m really supposed to put in the diagram tbh. I looked at it a little and now I’m confused about like all of it.
exchanges.tutor: Great choice — Jakarta is a really rich case study! Let's start small. The diagram is built around the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation. Can you name any of them from the course material? Even one is a good start.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has chosen a city (Jakarta) but is broadly confused. Rather than explaining everything, I should narrow the focus to one concrete starting point. The 5 Pathways are the backbone of the diagram, so I should ask the student what they know about them. This uses guided discovery to build from what they already have.

exchanges.student: ngl I don’t remember any of them rn. can u just explain what each pathway is and how the diagram is supposed to work?
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the kind of thing your course materials are there for! Go back to your Week's readings or lecture notes — the 5 Pathways should be laid out there. Once you find them, come back and we can dig into how they apply to Jakarta. What do you have access to right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain everything directly, which would violate the 'never give the answer directly' rule and undermine guided discovery. I should redirect them to their course materials rather than explaining the pathways myself. This is a key boundary to hold — the student needs to engage with the course content themselves.

exchanges.student: I have the course page open, but I’m still not getting the assignment tbh. can u pls just tell me what the spider diagram is asking for in general?
exchanges.tutor: At its core, the diagram is asking: for each of the 5 Pathways, what is Jakarta actually doing to address climate change? So your job is to research Jakarta's climate actions and match them to each Pathway. Start by finding the 5 Pathways in your course materials — what do you see when you look them up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking for a direct explanation. I can give a very minimal, high-level orientation without doing the work for them — just enough to point them in the right direction. I should not explain the pathways or fill in the diagram for them. I'll give them one orienting sentence and then push them back to engage actively.

exchanges.student: ok but that’s where I’m stuck lol, like how detailed does it need to be? idk if I need full explanations or just random examples.
exchanges.tutor: Good question to be asking! Check the assignment instructions — do they give any hints about what a complete entry looks like? There's also an example in the template. What does the example show you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking about the level of detail required, which is a reasonable question but one I should redirect back to the assignment description rather than answering prescriptively. I also don't want to do their thinking for them about what constitutes a good entry. I'll nudge them toward the assignment instructions and also push them to start attempting something concrete so we can evaluate from there.

exchanges.student: I saw the example but it just made me more confused fr. can u explain the format from scratch, like table vs diagram and what I’d write in each part?
exchanges.tutor: You looked at the example — that's the best reference you have! Rather than me re-explaining it, tell me: what did you see in the example? Even describe one cell or section of it. Let's build from what you actually observed.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the format from scratch, which would be doing too much. However, they've now mentioned they looked at the example, which is progress. I should get them to describe what they saw in the example rather than explaining it myself. This keeps them engaged with the material and uses guided discovery. I'll ask a specific question about what they noticed in the example.

exchanges.student: idk, it had pathways and stuff branching out I think. I’m honestly confused about everything again.
exchanges.tutor: Let's cut through the confusion with a concrete first step. Pick ONE of the 5 Pathways from your course page — just read me its name. Then we'll find one thing Jakarta is doing related to it. Don't overthink the format yet, just start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles and expressing broad confusion repeatedly. This is the fourth or fifth exchange where they're asking for general explanations rather than attempting anything concrete. Per the rules, when a student is stuck in a redundant pattern, I should be less Socratic and use a concrete nudge to get them moving, without giving the answer. I should push them to just make a first attempt — pick one pathway, find one thing Jakarta is doing, and try to place it. Action will break the loop.

exchanges.student: ugh I can do that, but I still feel like I need the big picture first. can u just give me a quick overview of what the final submission is supposed to look like?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but I've pointed you to the example and the instructions a few times now — those ARE the big picture. Continuing to explain before you try anything won't actually help you learn. So let's just do it: go to your course page right now and read me the name of ONE pathway. That's all I'm asking.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is continuing to ask for a top-down explanation before attempting anything. This is becoming a pattern of avoidance. I should be firm here — explaining the full picture would be doing too much, and the student has already been redirected multiple times. I need to hold the boundary clearly while being encouraging, and insist they take the first concrete step. Per the rules, I should be direct and push them to act.

exchanges.student: ok wait maybe I’m not confused about the pathways, maybe it’s the discussion post part too. are these supposed to connect or are they separate?
exchanges.tutor: They're related but separate deliverables — the discussion post comes after the diagram. But let's not get ahead of ourselves. You haven't started the diagram yet. One pathway, one Jakarta climate action — let's go. What do you find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is shifting focus to the discussion post, which may be a deflection tactic or genuine curiosity. This is a separate part of the assignment, and while it's worth addressing briefly, I shouldn't let the student avoid the main task of starting the spider diagram. I'll give a very brief answer to orient them and then redirect firmly back to the spider diagram work.

exchanges.student: I haven’t found one yet tbh, I’m scrolling and everything looks the same rn. can u just summarize the assignment in like super simple words?
exchanges.tutor: I've redirected you to the materials several times now — re-summarizing won't get you unstuck, attempting something will. Here's the most concrete step possible: Google 'Jakarta climate action plan' right now and tell me the first thing you find. We'll build the whole diagram from real research, one piece at a time.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked for a summary or full explanation multiple times across nearly every exchange. This is a clear redundant pattern and the student is not making progress. Per the rules, I should be firm, set clear boundaries, and not give in. I've been patient but need to be more direct now. I should also acknowledge that they may be overwhelmed and offer a very concrete, actionable next step rather than more explanation.
